author: @boogieknee3781
text:
Rob.I think you missed the point of making it from paper.
If the paper version was made from simple modular pieces...anybody with a sheet materal.....
1.reclaimed plywood
2.reclaimed pvc
3.(fill this space from your recycling bin)
can make one.
A cleverer design(since turbines themselves  incur the wrath of the local council planning) would be one that hugs a wall or a fence.
(The outer side vented,the bottom given a turbine outlet)
Running the dc directly into a sand battery inside the target buildings floor during cold weather and using a simple switch to direct it to other uses* in the summer seems the cheapest solution.
*diy peltier fridge is what I chose to build
As exciting as 3d printers are,they are expensive to buy and maintain for any non engineer.
We are fortunate that we have one at the local library,but the cost is prohibitively expensive for my neighbours.
Hence I regularly build/repair stuff for them using recycled materials,simply to stop them dumping 'fixables' nto landfill.

author: @Sevententh
text: Hi, I found this very interesting, and thought about your design. Could you design as a single circular piece and stack?  If anyone is interested: Use a block (3mm x 50mm x 190mm) and duplicate 3x with a rotation offset by -45, 45, 90deg (each centred 100,100). Now add 2 pyramids (175mm & 165mm width), both with a height of 150mm (change height to change the angle/pitch), (centre 100,100). The inner pyramid change to a hole. A large box to cover the whole area and move vertically up 50mm. Select all and group. This would give you an overall height of 50mm, which could be stacked easy. The bottom pyramid would be thinner at the bottom, compared to the top - creating a directional funnel. Shame I can't add a picture to a comment...

author: @stephenshepherd3171
text:
Interesting idea. I just saw the first one, and them saw there was an update. Have you thought about having a flue pointing upwards for each segment? This one appears to have six segments..Therefore you have no loss for the wind blowing straight through and out the other side. You should catch the wind on three sides at a time.
Perhaps with a slight spiral in the flue to assist in the direction to get the most efficiency out of the motor?
But only a suggestion. This is still a good idea of yours.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: Robert, doesn't the wind 'lose' energy by making the 90 degree direction change?

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Ah, no. I see what you mean, but most loses would be from friction, eddy currents, and some pressure differences caused by obstruction; the '90 turning' has little effect as conservation of momentum makes it all come out in the wash, so to speak.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 so the losses would be there.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: @justtinkering6713 Not in the '90' itself.

author: @jasonmorello1374
text: personally, i would have made such self stacking pieces go in a half length offset, similar to brickwork. gather minimum winds, channel them upwards, would still be fine. Might have larger vent passes, allowing a short distance laminar flow into the middle perhaps and low speeds. It does help a lot with power transfer between the air(fluid) and the final capture(propeller/turbine), as the more laminar the flow the less eddy current energy losses. Also, offset stacking is generally more structurally strong. All would be needed is a mid structure rib.

Keep your great work up!

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering agreed.

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
Many homes have an unused chimney, as a result of new smoke free zone laws. I think these could be used to add a heat pump air condition system to  older homes. What is required is a concentric air duct pipe passed up the flue. A fire place located heat pump could then be used to exchange hot and cold air taken from roof top level. An inverted version of this passive Darwin  wind turbine could direct fresh air down the flue, while the more usual configuration could be used to suck up stale air, and discharge it upwards. 
Potentially this could cool homes in summer and warm them in winter.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Yeah, but they were more gimmick than effective, the down-draughts being more a product of cold chimney and roof induced eddy currents. Once the chimney warmed the problem pretty much disappeared anyway. Try seeing the strength of draw in a warm chimney by blocking it with a bit of board, it's more than you'd expect.

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
@bikerfirefarter7280I think there main purpose is to stop smoke blowing back into the room when the fire is first lit, before the fires heat generates a significant up draft. 
 open fireplaces are become more difficult to find now days. We live in a smokeless zone, do our two fireplaces are now obsolete. 
I do remember as a child,  using a newspaper to help a fire to draw. It was not uncommon for the paper to be sucked into the fireplace, and catch fire. 

I am interested in using these redundant fireplaces and flues as a duct for exchanging air with the outside world. Taking air from roof top level has some advantages. A one stage one of our chimney flues was converted to an outlet for the central heating system, this involved feeding a flexible aluminium pipe liner up the flue. It was this that gave me the idea of using such a liner to feed air to a heat exchanger. As an RF system designer, I worked with large heliax cable, this used a spiral PTFE separator, to keep the central conductor away from the cable metal outer screen. The cable was then insulated and cooled with a flow of pressurised nitrogen gas. 
It occurred to me that the idea of the PTFE separator could be used to make two concentric pipes into a chimney liner, providing a way to combine both an inlet and outlet pipe, venting into a modified chimney pot. The air flow could then be used to feed a small heat exchanger in the fireplace. With the intention of providing efficient ventilation. I surmised that the air flow might be adequate, given that a fire place is a significant source of drafts in the home, often requiring a flue baffle door to be fitted, to allow the flue to be closed.

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
Do you loose air pressure through the open slots on the sides not facing the wind? 
If you put all the slots on one side, covering up to 180 degrees, a solid back side, and a “weathervane” to keep the device always facing the wind you may capture more of the winds energy. The base and top would both have to be circular so the device and the blades could rotate. This does create a moving part, but I doubt it would add much noise.

author: @douglaswebber660
text: Love your videos. Wish I had the space and resources to make what I want but I can give you my idea… does different resident frequencies change the way aluminum works inside the magnetic fields

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no idea mate

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
There are definitely effects with resonant frequencies regarding inductance and capacitance in various cores that impact on efficiency, at higher power core saturation plays a big part too, depending on core material/structure. I'm not sure of the effects of Aluminium though. It's worth looking into, I expect there is plenty of research available.
Make a simple frequency generator, hook it to a coil and a composite core(s), and measure the impedence. Isolate the effects of 2PifC and 1/2PifL and you should have your answer. An oscilloscope will be invaluable, but digital/computer scopes are quite cheap these days, they are even do-able with Arduino and Raspberry.

author: @Boblee84
text: Awesome to see your dabbling with Tinkercad, its actually a really great program once you get to know it. I have been printing for about 6 years now and I still use tinkercad on the regular. I have designed everything I have needed with it and find it much quicker then other more complex programs. After 6 years of using it my top suggestions would be to use the favorites tab and spend some time exploring all the shape menu's. When you find a useful shape then click the little star and it will be in your favorites menu so you don't have to search it again later. Also I highly recommend the shape generator tab for more complex designs as there are more parameters to set on the object menu, with most of the items, so they can be manipulated to create certain shapes like Archimedes screw's, airfoil designs, pipe's, funnels, gears, and a multitude of higher resolution options for cylinders and what not. Hope it helps! Tinker away👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tips mate - thank you for sharing - cheers

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
Nice idea from Max. Rather makes redundant an idea I was having about improving the original, but that's a good thing.
My concept was based on, as the upward flowing air moves faster it's lower pressured so if a flap also partially opened the flap either side of it then that should allow some extra air to go be sucked through the system as a whole. 
I suspect this new design does that, with no extra moving parts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I liked it a lot mate - clever thinking from Max - but don't give up on your design just yet - who knows what you will come up with - it is often the process of doing that sparks ideas

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I had a little thought about your version of Max's design. 
As you have the buttresses on the outside it might be worth making them grooved, at an upwards angle, that way as the air hits them it starts to channel the flow in the direction required, smoothing out the turbulent flow at the edges. Probably only make a minor difference but, if being 3D printed, only small amount of extra effort at the design stage.

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text: Ohh actually thinking about that makes me also think, going back to the original Darwin design, adding progressively deeper grooves on the outside face of the flaps would allow extra air to be dragged in without adding any extra complicated moving parts :-)

text: You are an endless well of generator ideas. I'm curious. Have you given thought to a steam powered generator. Recently watched a How it's made episode for a small radial steam engine. author: @lagunafishing
text: Anyone familiar with mould making could simply replicate any number of parts from just one print saving tons of time.. A basic RTV mould making kit consists of a silicone or flexible polyurethane mould into which you pour resin and hardener. Apart from saving lots of time printing, the parts can be made to any shore hardness to suit your project.

replies.author: @stewiex
replies.text:
I used to make industrial Polyurethane and now have six 3d printers. You're not wrong, except you can only make parts while you're awake and you typically have a lot of preparation and post processing you have to do on the parts. There are also limiting factors like total curing time and space to have many molds at once.

I think another solution could be to print parts with a 3d printer and then fiberglass them afterwards to give them a tough exoskeleton.

replies.author: @raven_talionis
replies.text: @stewiex you can even do a process similar to fiberglass with resin, a paintbrush and regular fabric. Of course it won't be the exact same properties as fiberglass, but it's probably cheaper and at least a little safer than fiberglass.

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: @stewiex I've been making moulds, designing and casting parts for over 50 years. My method of spin casting hundreds of parts per hour is second to none. Apart from cutting off a spur, there is no post processing involved, whereas all 3D printed parts involve some kind of finishing. 3D printing is mainly for making one off prototypes, it is not intended for mass producing duplicate parts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it doesn't save time mate - I get it you think it does but you are only thinking of you - imagine the beginner - also have you done much 3d printing recently - things have changed - I just pluck the parts from the bed and use them!

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yes I get it, but I'm not just thinking of me lol, the suggestion was meant to be helpful to beginners. I have done lots of 3D printing which are fine for knocking out a few prototype designs but casting your creations from a master saves a ton of time - depending how many you need of course.

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text:
Very cool, was just playing around with blender for CAD and its kind of tough, fushion 360 looks easier and this   looks just as user friendly option to try.  I just ordered my first 3d printer last night after weeks of shopping it seems, having my mind set on this one then that one, with so many options I  ended going with the best deal i found which was an Artillery Hornet for $89 canadian fund shipped to my door from ali express.  Which is like what 50 pounds?  in 2-7 days no less because they have stock in the USA and dont have to wait a month for china delivery.
  So i got 3, without even looking these printers up much, and i did so much research before this which kind of felt like a waste going into these ones blind, but after a few youtube reviews ive heard nothing but good things, especially in the comments which is more important than the review itself i think.  And this is when they were selling for over $200 USD, so at amost 1/4 the cost i think it was a no brainer. Id love to print these wind direction thingys because the only issue about the hornet was its lack of cooling, although it has 2 fans, they're arent angled optimaly perhaps and something like this would be cool to try and fit it in the housing.   
Ultimately a solidstate fan is the best solution i think and they've just been revealed at CES, PCworld has a spot on them and they're pretty remarkable tech.  I wonder if this could be applied to power generation because a fan is just a power generator in reverse.  These solid state fans looks like flat lithium battery packs that suck air in one side and blow it out high speeds on the other. Many tiny vibrating membranes , vibrating at supersonic speeds is the jist of how they work.  No moving parts, apart from the membranes insided the sealed container.  Offers huge coooling at low power and small form factor i think will be a game changer in 3d printing, who right now is comming up  a gainst a thermal wall when it comes to printing speed, sort of like computers.    Frore Systems is the comapny that makes these that look like they're ready to be used now.  Marketed for thin laptops, which cant reach maximum performance because of thermal limits, i think 3d printing could see improvements now however.   These hornets print head  looks perfect to slide one of these solidstate fan into, that combined with one of these darwin turbine direction things, could be a winner, 1000 m/s printing with no quality loss!!  And soon we're printing solar sails on the moon in 0g further aiding print speeds and cold fusing metal , printing metal, and we're off to andromeda in no time, perhaps with some vibrating membranes propelling us to near light speeds and toward our next door neighbour solar system.   
But I digress,  I think the hornets have been discounted so much because they couldnt compete with the model above them for just a few dollars more - the Artillery genius so they've gone unsold and now being discounted.  At leaste that was one of the only few criticisms about this printer i remember reading  "why buy this when for $20 more i can get the artilliery genius"  but thats not the case anymore so... i'll have to wait to see how they turn out.  But I also really appreciate their unique design on the bowden tube assembly, which tells me they have some real thinkers there and not just some guys slapping off the shelfs parts together and calling it a printer. That being said anything is good these days It seems, and ive heard nothing but good things about the elegoo's new lineup which had them on my list instead of sovol after hearin they just had creality parts inside, although arranged better, i appreciated the uniquness of Artillery Hornet.

author: @MerwinARTist
text: I really enjoy watching this tutorial in using the CAD software.  It's always a huge learning curve to pick up something new and figure out what all the bells and whistles do.  As you finished with this demonstration .. I realized it could be done as a flat wall too .. then you could put one of your blue turbines in a lateral position up top.  Of course the purpose of your design here is to take advantage of wind direction changes.  It's all good!  🙂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering First: I LOOOVE this content. Actually showing the design process can teach a lot about both the maker and the tool 😉
Right in the middle of the clip i googled "tincercad" and saved a link for later ;)

Just one tip i learned long ago in such programs. This part is symmetrical, so you could save almost half of work if you draw only half of the blade and then mirrored it.

Keep your enthusiasm levels. Your joy of tinkering is our motivation to tinker even more😁Just as you said: "it's all about making stuff". And i agree with my whole heart.
Now i'm off to fix my friends electric heater (those dusty short circuits are horrible) then to fix my gf's earphones (exchanging broken spekars) and then to the kitchen to make a stew 😁

author: @timothyneiswander3151
text: I watch 3D printing videos sometimes because I have been trying to talk myself into buying one. I came across a video where they recycled plastic bottles into the 3D printer. I thought it would be a good way to keep costs down. Sorry I don't have a link. I must not have saved it.

replies.author: @stl7694
replies.text: Recycling plastic bottles into filament is miserable.  The spools seem expensive but really last longer than you think.  3d printers are okay, but you might consider just paying for someone to print it and send it to you.  If your not sure what you will want to make it is a good option.

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: It's doable and quite easy to convert the bottles into filament with a certain type of cheap glue gun. However since it's not professional grade, the diameter of the filament will be uneven and cause clogging etc in the extruder way more often than standard filament rolls.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am thinking of a filament extruder at sometime mate - but one step at a time I think

author: @deanallenjones
text:
I've been using tinkercad for a while, and yet I still learnt something new in yesterdays lesson :)
I had a class of kids design  a lightsabre hilt by committee and then printed it out in their lunch break, They loved it :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is awesome mate - I am tempted to do that myself lol

replies.author: @deanallenjones
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering It's amazing how well you can get 30 kids working together.
"Show of hands, useful thing or toy/decoration"
"show of hands sci-fi, fantasy, real life thing for a doll"....
I used an ender3 with a 0.8 nozzle to do a rough copy in the lunch time and then sent a few made with a regular nozzle into the school a few days later for them to decorate in art class.
Kids can be amazingly creative and smart when given the chance and some inspiration:)
Thanks for you videos (the next day I work at the museum, we're making a generator!"

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: @deanallenjones Sounds like a really awesome experience to teach kids about 3D printers and 3D CAD modelling. You're among the first people in history to do so I reckon.

replies.author: @deanallenjones
replies.text:
@DKFX1 I'm sure others did it way before me. But I only had the kids for a day (I visit schools and museums doing science and magic shows)
I had the idea of group voting on things as I narrow down the options after seeing a comedy music act use the same system to have 200 people play D and D with them at a gig

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: @deanallenjonesI think only in recent years has 3D modelling and 3D printing become user-friendly and consumer friendly  to a point that kids could be reasonably engaged in the process, so yes you're probably not the first, but certainly among the first few people doing it.
